case_name: Northshore Parts LLC v. Riverton Robotics, Inc.
court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois

jurisdiction.venue_basis: Diversity jurisdiction; amount in controversy exceeds $75,000; parties are citizens of different states (IL and WI)

procedural_posture: Cross-motions for summary judgment on contract formation and terms; Riverton moves to compel arbitration; Northshore moves to deny arbitration and for partial summary judgment on nonconformity.
governing_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-SIGN), 15 U.S.C. § 7001 et seq.
Illinois Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), 815 ILCS 333/10 et seq.

timeline.event: Video call between procurement manager at Northshore and sales manager at Riverton about servo motors.

Riverton email to Northshore: "I will sell 1,000 units of SRV-200 servo motors at $95 each, delivery by May 15, FOB Milwaukee. Please review and place the order through our secure portal." Email includes portal link.
Northshore reply email: "Looks good, confirm and send docs."

Northshore uses portal link; checkout page shows: product 'SRV-200', quantity 1000, unit price $95, subtotal $95,000; beneath order summary is hyperlinked text: "By clicking 'Place Order', you accept the Terms of Sale." The link opens a modal with full Terms, including Section 11 (Arbitration) and specification: Model SRV-200, IP65 protection rating.
Northshore clicks 'Place Order'. No separate checkbox appears; assent recorded by click. System logs capture user, timestamp, and IP address.
Northshore emails Riverton: "We need to add overnight shipping and change to IP67 spec; confirm."

Riverton acknowledges order as placed for SRV-200 IP65 units; says overnight shipping may be possible at extra charge; states IP67 upgrade is not available for SRV-200, suggests different model with price increase. No express acceptance of IP67 change.
Riverton ships 1,000 SRV-200 motors with IP65 rating; delivery occurs 2026-05-05. Packing list matches SRV-200 IP65.
Northshore inspects and claims nonconformity because parts are IP65 rather than requested IP67; refuses to pay; demands return authorization and cover costs to source IP67.
Riverton issues invoice for $95,000 and demands payment; invokes Terms of Sale Section 11 (arbitration) and moves to compel arbitration.

undisputed_points: Both parties are merchants under the Illinois UCC.
Northshore clicked 'Place Order' on Riverton's portal.
The Terms of Sale included an arbitration clause in Section 11 and specified SRV-200 with IP65 protection rating.
The email from Riverton on 2026-03-02 contained item, quantity, price, delivery date, and FOB term.
Riverton shipped SRV-200 IP65 units by May 15 and tendered delivery.
disputed_points: Whether Riverton's 2026-03-02 email was an offer or an invitation to negotiate.
Whether the arbitration clause and IP65 specification became part of the parties' contract.
Whether Northshore's 2026-03-02 14:10 email modifying specs to IP67 effected a contract modification.
Whether the delivered goods were nonconforming under the agreed specifications.

documents.name: Checkout Page (Portal)
key_terms: Hyperlink: "By clicking 'Place Order', you accept the Terms of Sale"
Terms modal includes: Section 11 Arbitration (AAA, Wisconsin seat), Limitation of liability (price paid cap), Specifications: SRV-200 IP65

northshore.theory: Cover and consequential damages due to alleged nonconforming goods (IP67 needed for end-customer contract).
northshore.amount: Estimated $40,000 cover differential and $25,000 consequential losses (disclaimed by Terms).

riverton.theory: Contract price $95,000 plus costs and fees; arbitration compelled per Terms.
riverton.amount: $95,000 invoice plus any collection costs permitted by Terms.

issues: Whether the parties formed a binding contract for 1,000 SRV-200 units and on what terms.
Whether Riverton's online Terms of Sale (including arbitration and limitation of liability) became part of the contract via clickwrap.
Whether Northshore's post-click email requesting IP67 constituted a contract modification requiring Riverton's express assent.
Whether shipment of SRV-200 IP65 satisfied the agreed specifications, and whether Northshore may reject or revoke acceptance under Article 2.

candidate_authorities.title: Midwest Machining, Inc. v. Phoenix Components, Inc.
candidate_authorities.proposition: An email that states the item, quantity, price, and delivery date is sufficiently definite to constitute an offer; acceptance may be manifested electronically when the offeree takes an affirmative action to assent that is reasonably attributable to it.

candidate_authorities.title: Lakeview Tech, Inc. v. Greenline Supply Co.
candidate_authorities.proposition: Clickwrap terms are enforceable when conspicuously presented proximate to the acceptance action and assent is captured by an unambiguous click; browsewrap without affirmative assent is insufficient.

candidate_authorities.title: Nationwide E-Transactions, Inc. v. Harper
candidate_authorities.proposition: Under the E-SIGN Act, electronic signatures and records cannot be denied legal effect solely because they are in electronic form; a recorded click can constitute a signature if attributable to the party.

candidate_authorities.title: Riviera Tools, LLC v. Benton Manufacturing Corp.
candidate_authorities.court: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit
candidate_authorities.proposition: Between merchants under UCC § 2-207, arbitration and limitation-of-liability clauses are presumptively material alterations that require express assent to become part of the contract when added in an acceptance or confirmation.

candidate_authorities.title: Brightline Auto Group, Inc. v. West Loop Motors, LLC
candidate_authorities.proposition: Hyperlinked terms positioned below the acceptance button and not requiring a click-through may be insufficient to bind a consumer; emphasizes clear notice and manifestation of assent.

candidate_authorities.title: Pacific Gadgets Co. v. SilverTech Ltd.
candidate_authorities.court: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit
candidate_authorities.proposition: Affirmed enforcement of clickwrap terms where the terms were conspicuous and the user was required to take an affirmative action acknowledging the terms.

candidate_authorities.title: Metro Components, Inc. v. Sunline Imports, LLC
candidate_authorities.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of New York
candidate_authorities.proposition: Limitation-of-liability clause added in a post-negotiation confirmation was a material alteration under § 2-207 and did not become part of the contract absent express assent.

candidate_authorities.title: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 24
candidate_authorities.proposition: An offer is a manifestation of willingness to enter into a bargain, so made as to justify another in understanding that his assent is invited and will conclude it.

formation_paths: Path A (Seller Offer): Riverton’s email constituted an offer; the portal click accepted the offer (including offer-incorporated Terms). Arbitration and IP65 spec included as offer terms.
Path B (Portal Offer): Riverton’s portal and Terms were the offer; Northshore’s click accepted; arbitration and IP65 spec included.

Path C (Battle of Forms): If Northshore’s 10:05 email is construed as an offer and the portal click is a confirmation with additional terms, § 2-207 analysis applies; arbitration and limitation are material alterations requiring express assent.
analysis_hints.modification: Northshore’s 14:10 email requesting IP67 is a proposed modification under § 2-209 requiring Riverton’s assent; absent assent, original IP65 spec governs.
analysis_hints.remedies: If IP65 spec governs, goods are conforming and buyer owes price; if contract required IP67, buyer may reject or cover; consequential damages may be barred by limitation-of-liability if enforceable.

requested_relief.riverton: Order compelling arbitration and staying/dismissing the case; alternatively, summary judgment for the price.
requested_relief.northshore: Order denying arbitration; declaratory judgment that arbitration and limitation clauses are not part of the contract; damages for cover and losses.

notes_for_brief_writer: Seat in Illinois federal court; apply Illinois substantive law under Erie; federal law governs the effect of arbitration clauses under the FAA but state-law formation controls.
Assume both parties are sophisticated merchants; evaluate conspicuousness and assent on the portal.
Do not cite real cases beyond the authorities listed here; keep analysis hypothetical and consistent with provided authorities.
